ontology.foodex2_description: Gnocchi — pasta-type dish based on potato dough, boiled and served with sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated multi-sauce gnocchi composite code; A00GJ (pasta dishes) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for potato gnocchi dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: basil pesto (pine nuts, basil, Parmesan, olive oil, garlic)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of potato gnocchi with sauce, approximately 300 g, as typically served in a Singaporean Italian restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Gnocchi, potato, cooked and Pasta dishes composite reference
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked potato gnocchi and representative Italian cheese-based sauces (pesto, tomato-basil, quattro formaggi). The input record provided averaged values across three sauce variants at confidence 0.7; these have been retained and cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy data for plausibility. Values represent a weighted average across the three sauce options as described in the dish description.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for potato gnocchi with mixed Italian sauces was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 68 is assigned based on the closest analogous food: cooked potato gnocchi, which has a reported GI of approximately 68 (medium) per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283), consistent with the high starch content of potato-wheat flour dough. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 54.99) / 100 ≈ 37.3 for a 300 g serving (18.33 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3).

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g exceeds the AHA 5 g/100 g threshold; sodium 316.7 mg/100 g is borderline elevated; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less, request reduced-cheese preparation, and avoid the Quattro Formaggi sauce option, which contributes the highest saturated fat and sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic index approximately 68 (medium-high) with 18.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 1.3 g dietary fibre per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 55 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of 37
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should restrict gnocchi portions to 100–150 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables to reduce glycemic load, and prefer the Pomodoro sauce over Quattro Formaggi to minimise concurrent saturated fat intake that may worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g and trans fat 0.17 g/100 g from multiple cheeses and cream; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 18 g saturated fat, exceeding the AHA daily limit of 13 g for a 2000 kcal diet
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid the Quattro Formaggi sauce and limit total serving to 150 g or less; substituting the cream-based sauce with Pomodoro e Basilico significantly reduces saturated fat and trans fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g from multiple cheeses and cream exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; tomato and potato are nightshade ingredients that may trigger joint symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request tomato-free and reduced-cheese preparation, consider cauliflower gnocchi as a nightshade-free alternative, and retain olive oil and pine nuts for their anti-inflammatory MUFA content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Potato Gnocchi with Italian Sauce is a classic Italian pasta-style dish consisting of soft, pillowy dumplings made from mashed potato, wheat flour, egg, and salt, boiled until tender and then pan-tossed with one of three classic Italian sauces: Pesto Genovese (basil, pine nuts, Parmesan, olive oil, garlic — vegetarian), Pomodoro e Basilico (tomato, basil, olive oil — vegetarian and vegan), or Quattro Formaggi (Gorgonzola, Taleggio, Parmesan, mozzarella, cream — vegetarian). The texture is soft and slightly chewy with a mild potato flavour that absorbs the sauce well. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 217 kcal per 100 g, with 7.3 g protein, 12.7 g fat (of which 6.0 g is saturated), and 18.3 g carbohydrate with only 1.3 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 316.7 mg per 100 g. A standard 300 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 650 kcal, 55 g carbohydrate, and 950 mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 68 (medium-high), making this dish a concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The high saturated fat content from cheese and cream raises cardiovascular and hypertension concerns. The dish is vegetarian across all sauce variants but contains gluten, dairy, egg, and tree nuts (pine nuts). It is not suitable for vegan diets except when ordered with the Pomodoro e Basilico sauce. Commonly served as a main course in Italian restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: What is the glycemic index of gnocchi?
Can I eat gnocchi if I have high blood pressure?
What sauce is lowest in calories for gnocchi?
How much sodium is in gnocchi?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked potato gnocchi and representative Italian sauces. The input record averaged values across three sauce variants at confidence 0.7 (ai_generated, grabfood provenance). Values are plausible and cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy but are not sourced from an exact single-dish entry in a primary approved database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this multi-sauce composite gnocchi dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the input record and is not available from a single approved-source entry for this exact preparation. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite multi-sauce gnocchi dish are not available in a single approved-source entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. Input record provenance is ai_generated from grabfood; nutrient values have been retained as plausible and cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy. The dish description references three sauce options; nutrient values represent an average across all three variants. The ethnic_tags field in the input contained 'Pizza' and 'Chicken Dish' which are clearly erroneous for this record and have been excluded. country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore, reflecting the restaurant location rather than the dish's culinary origin (Italy); region_of_origin has been corrected to Italy.
